coredata.dc:title: Ultrasensitive determination of receptor tyrosine kinase with a label-free electrochemical immunosensor using graphene quantum dots-modified screen-printed electrodes
coredata.dc:creator: Mollarasouli, Fariba
Serafín, Verónica
Campuzano, Susana
Yáñez-Sedeño, Paloma
Pingarrón, José M.
Asadpour-Zeynali, Karim
coredata.dc:description: A new label-free electrochemical immunosensor is constructed for the selective and sensitive determination of the clinically relevant biomarker receptor tyrosine kinase (AXL) in human serum. The disposable immunosensing platform is prepared by immobilization of the specific anti-AXL antibody onto amine functionalized graphene quantum dots (fGQDs)-modified screen-printed carbon electrodes (SPCEs). The affinity reactions were monitored by measuring the decrease in the differential pulse voltammetric (DPV) response of the redox probe Fe(CN)6 3−/4−. All the experimental variables involved in the preparation of the modified electrodes and in the immunosensor performance were optimized. The as prepared immunosensor exhibits an improved analytical performance with respect to other electrochemical immunosensors reported so far, with a wider range of linearity and a lower detection limit, 0.5 pg mL−1, which is more than one hundred thousand times lower than the established cut-off value for heart failure (HF) diagnosis in serum (71 ng mL−1). The developed immunosensor was successfully applied to the determination of the endogenous content of AXL in serum of HF patients without any matrix effect observed after just a sample dilution.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs)
Label-free
Electrochemical immunosensor
AXL
Serum
Screen-printed carbon electrode (SPCE)

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst orcid primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination of AXL with the anti-AXL-fGQDs/SPCE immunosesnor
Characterization and surface morphology of GQDs
Optimization of the experimental variables involved in the preparation of the immunosensor
Analytical characteristics for the determination of AXL achieved with the AXL-anti-AXL-fGQDs/SPCE immunosensor
Determination of sAXL in human serum

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Financial support from the University of Tabriz, Tabriz, Iran and of projects: Retos Colaboración RTC-2015-4184-1 (cofinanced by the Ministry of Economy and Competitivity and FEDER “una manera de hacer Europa”), CTQ2015-70023-R and CTQ2015-64402-C2-1-R (Spanish Ministry of Economy and Competitivity Research Projects) and S2013/MT-3029 (NANOAVANSENS Program from the Comunidad de Madrid) are gratefully acknowledged. The authors would also like to acknowledge Drs. M. Sabaté, M. Batlle and P. García de Frutos and the Hospital Clinic de Barcelona for kindly providing the serum samples from HF patients. Fariba Mollarasouli would like to thank her family for their valuable help and great effort.

Financial support from the University of Tabriz, Tabriz, Iran and of projects: Retos Colaboración RTC-2015-4184-1 (cofinanced by the Ministry of Economy and Competitivity and FEDER “una manera de hacer Europa”), CTQ2015-70023-R and CTQ2015-64402-C2-1-R ( Spanish Ministry of Economy and Competitivity Research Projects ) and S2013/MT-3029 (NANOAVANSENS Program from the Comunidad de Madrid ) are gratefully acknowledged. The authors would also like to acknowledge Drs. M. Sabaté, M. Batlle and P. García de Frutos and the Hospital Clinic de Barcelona for kindly providing the serum samples from HF patients. Fariba Mollarasouli would like to thank her family for their valuable help and great effort.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic display of the different steps involved in the construction of the label-free immunosensor for AXL involving NH2-fGQDs modified SPCEs and covalent immobilization of anti-AXL through the oxidized sugar chains.
Characterization of synthetized GQDs: TEM (A and B), FESEM (C) images, elemental mapping (D and E) and EDX analysis (F).
Cyclic voltammograms recorded during the electropolymerization process of 2-ABA at GQDs/SPCE. Inset: the magnification of the circled part.

Cyclic voltammograms (A,C) and Nyquist plots (B,D) recorded at SPCE (blue), GQDs/SPCE (orange), fGQDs/SPCE (green), anti-AXL-fGQDs/SPCE (grey) and anti-AXL-fGQDs/SPCE blocked with BSA in the absence (purple) and in the presence (pink) of 250 pg mL−1 AXL in a 5 mM Fe(CN)6 3− /4- and 0.1 M KCl solution. Inset of Fig. 4B: Equivalent circuit applied to fit impedance measurements where Rs is the resistance of electrolyte solution; Rct is the charge transfer resistance, W is the Warburg impedance, and CPE is the constant phase element. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Calibration plot for AXL obtained with the AXL-anti-AXL-fGQDs/SPCE immunosensor. Inset: DPV responses recorded in a 5 mM Fe(CN)6 3− /4- and 0.1 M KCl solution with the immunosensor in the presence of 0 and 250 pg mL−1 AXL. Error bars were estimated as triple of the standard deviation (n = 3).

Determination of the sAXL concentration (in ng mL−1) in human serum samples from five different HF patients (n = 3) with anti-AXL/NH2-fGQDs/SPCEs immunosensors and comparison with the results obtained with a commercial ELISA spectrophotometric kit.

head.ce:title: Ultrasensitive determination of receptor tyrosine kinase with a label-free electrochemical immunosensor using graphene quantum dots-modified screen-printed electrodes

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, Tabriz 51666-16471, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Departamento de Química Analítica, Facultad de Ciencias Químicas, Universidad Complutense de Madrid, 28040 Madrid, Spain

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: IMDEA Nanoscience, Ciudad Universitaria de Cantoblanco, 28049 Madrid, Spain

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Departamento de Química Analítica, Facultad de Ciencias Químicas, Universidad Complutense de Madrid, 28040 Madrid, Spain.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A new label-free electrochemical immunosensor is constructed for the selective and sensitive determination of the clinically relevant biomarker receptor tyrosine kinase (AXL) in human serum. The disposable immunosensing platform is prepared by immobilization of the specific anti-AXL antibody onto amine functionalized graphene quantum dots (fGQDs)-modified screen-printed carbon electrodes (SPCEs). The affinity reactions were monitored by measuring the decrease in the differential pulse voltammetric (DPV) response of the redox probe Fe(CN)6 3−/4−. All the experimental variables involved in the preparation of the modified electrodes and in the immunosensor performance were optimized. The as prepared immunosensor exhibits an improved analytical performance with respect to other electrochemical immunosensors reported so far, with a wider range of linearity and a lower detection limit, 0.5 pg mL−1, which is more than one hundred thousand times lower than the established cut-off value for heart failure (HF) diagnosis in serum (71 ng mL−1). The developed immunosensor was successfully applied to the determination of the endogenous content of AXL in serum of HF patients without any matrix effect observed after just a sample dilution.

ce:section.ce:para: Heart failure (HF) has a high impact on health systems and society as a whole because of its incidence, prevalence, and high mortality rate. The number of admissions due to HF has quadrupled in the last 30 years. Therefore, prognostic biomarkers able to be measured with minimally invasive methodologies are needed to improve the management of the HF epidemic. AXL is a receptor tyrosine kinase discovered by Bryan and co-workers in 1991. The proteolytically processed extracellular fraction of this protein (sAXL) is considered to be a relevant biomarker in cancer [1], inflammatory processes [2] and pathophysiology of HF [3]. The sAXL concentration in serum is elevated in HF patients compared to controls, with a cut-off value of 71 ng mL−1 being established [4]. Therefore, the determination of sAXL by means of simple, low cost and fast methodologies able to be easily integrated in point-of-care (POC) devices and direct applicability in body fluids is highly demanded. Several sandwich-type commercial ELISA kits using biotin-labeled secondary antibodies and streptavidin-peroxidase conjugates are available. These kits provide calibration plots for AXL in concentration ranges covering from some tens to several thousands of pg mL−1. Apart from the relatively poor sensitivity, ELISAs suffer from other important drawbacks such as the relatively long assay time, which in some cases takes more than five hours, and the poor reproducibility with relative standard deviation (RSD) values around 10% or higher. It is worth to mention that the only electrochemical immunoassays for sAXL existing so far have been reported by our group. The developed methods used sandwich-type configurations involving covalent immobilization of anti-AXL antibody on polypyrrole propionic acid-modified SPCEs [5], or carboxyl-functionalized magnetic micro- or nanoparticles [6] and amperometric transduction at SPCEs using the HRP/H2O2/HQ system.

As it is well-known, label-free electrochemical immunosensors have some advantages compared which the sandwich configurations, mainly in terms of protocols simplification and cost per assay. Label-free strategies allow reducing the number of steps involved in the assay avoiding the need for tedious immobilization processes and the use of secondary antibodies. However, the preparation of label-free immunosensors requires careful consideration of two important aspects such as the immobilization of large immunoreagent loadings without increasing the resistance of the electrode surface and with optimal orientation and spacing for an efficient immunoreaction, and the use of a powerful amplification for the electrochemical response.

Among the quantum dot nanostructures, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) (<20 nm) have demonstrated to be attractive materials with unique features such as good water solubility and large surface-to-volume ratio [7]. Compared to semiconducting quantum dots (QDs), GQDs are superior in biocompatibility and low toxicity [8]. Moreover, unlike carbon dots (CDs), the graphene structure inside the GQDs endows them some of the excellent properties of graphene [9]. Taking advantage of their electrochemical properties, similar to graphene, GQDs have been used as modifiers of the electrode surface [10] and as carrier tags for signal amplification [11] in the development of electrochemical immunosensors. The abundance of hydrophilic edges and the presence of functional groups (-OH, -COOH) can enhance the loading of immunoreagents on the GQDs surface thus greatly improving the sensitivity.

In this work, a new label-free electrochemical immunosensor for AXL using SPCEs modified with amino-group functionalized GQDs is reported. Functionalization of GQDs/SPCE was accomplished by electropolymerization with 2-aminobenzyl amine (2-ABA). The affinity reaction was monitored by measuring the decrease in the differential pulse voltammetric (DPV) responses of Fe(CN)6 3−/4− redox probe recorded at the modified SPCEs upon addition of AXL. The developed immunoplatform fulfilled the requirements of sensitivity, selectivity and reproducibility needed in clinical applications and was successfully employed for the determination of AXL in human sera from follow-up patients with heart diseases.

Voltammetric experiments were made with a PGSTAT101 potentiostat (Methrohm Autolab) using the Nova 1.8 electrochemical software (EcoChemie B.V.). Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy measurements were carried out with a μ-Autolab type III potentiostat (Ecochemie, The Netherlands) with FRA2 software. Screen-printed carbon electrodes (SPCEs) (DRP-110, DropSens) consisting of a 4-mm diameter carbon working electrode, a carbon counter electrode and an Ag pseudo-reference electrode were used as electrochemical transducers. A specific cable connector (DRP-CAC, DropSens, S.L.) acted as an interface between the potentiostat and the SPCE. All measurements were performed at room temperature.

Field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) of GQDs was carried out with a Tescan Mira3 FESEM equipped with gold coating and an EDX system. X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) was done with a Rigaku D/max-2200X-Ray Diffractometer (Bruker AXS) using a Cu Kα source (λ = 0.154056 nm). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) analysis was carried out using a Bruker FTIR spectrometer at room temperature (25 ± 1 °C). The photoluminescence (PL) spectra were recorded with a Shimadzu RF–540S fluorescence spectrometer. UV–visible studies were made with an Avantes diode array spectrophotometer. The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) characterization was performed using a Phillips CM 12 microscope operating at an accelerating voltage of 120 kV.

The main reagents, including citric acid, potassium ferro- and ferricyanide, 2-aminobenzyl amine (2-ABA) and sodium borohydride, were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Bovine serum albumin (BSA) was from Gerbu. Sodium chloride, potassium chloride, di-sodium hydrogen phosphate and sodium dihydrogen phosphate were from Scharlab. Anti-human AXL mouse monoclonal antibody (anti-AXL) and AXL were obtained from the Human Total AXL DuoSet®IC ELISA kit (R&D Systems, Inc. Catalog Number DYC 1643-2). Phosphate buffer saline (PBS) consisting of 0.1 M phosphate buffer and NaCl solutions with adjusted pH at 7.4 was used as a washing buffer and supporting electrolyte for preparing AXL solutions. All chemical reagents used were of analytical grade and all aqueous solutions were prepared with deionized water. Anti-AXL antibody solution was prepared in 0.1 mol L−1 PBS solution of pH 7.4 containing also 3 mg mL−1 sodium periodate (Sigma-Aldrich). Human cardiac troponin T (cTnT, Cat 8 T13, HyTest, Ltd.), human C-reactive protein (CRP, Audit Diagnostics), lipoprotein(a) (Lp(a), kindly provided from Audit Diagnostics), human interleukin 8 (IL-8, from IL-8 human Elipair Kit, Abcam, Cat. ab48483), human tumor necrosis factor alpha (TNF-α, from Duoset ELISA human TNF-α, R&D Systems, Inc. Catalog Number DY210-05) and human cardiac troponin I (cTnI, Life Diagnostics) were tested as potential interfering species to evaluate the selectivity of the developed immunosensor.

GQDs were synthesized by direct pyrolysis of citric acid (CA) according to the method described by Dong et al. [12] with slight modifications. Briefly, 2 g of CA were heated until the temperature reached 200 °C. After 5 min, the powder was gradually converted to a pale-yellow color liquid. This suspension is still heated for another 30 min (changing its color to orange) and then added dropwise to a 100 mL freshly prepared 10 mg mL−1 NaOH solution under continuous stirring. The synthesis of GQDs was completed by neutralizing solution to pH 7.0 by addition of diluted hydrochloric acid.

It is worth to mention that reproducible GQDs are synthesized by a careful control of the temperature, time and color of liquid in the citric acid pyrolysis step. If this step is made at higher temperature or during more time, a brownish color solution was obtained instead of orange liquor and graphene oxide was synthesized instead of GQDs.

The fundamentals of the developed immunosensor are illustrated in Fig. 1 . The method described by Mehta et al. [13] with slight changes was followed. First, the surface of SPCE was modified by casting a 5 μL aliquot of synthesized GQDs onto the working electrode surface of SPCE. Then, the modified electrode was maintained in an oven for 1 h at 120 °C to ensure adhesion GQDs to the SPCE surface. After cooling, the resulting GQDs/SPCE was functionalized by electropolymerization with 2-ABA. For this purpose, 40 μL of a 10 mg mL−1 2-ABA solution (prepared in 1.0 mol L−1 HCl) was dropped onto the electrode surface and the electropolymerization was performed by cyclic voltammetry (20 cycles at 200 mVs−1 from 0.0 to −1.0 V vs. Ag pseudo-reference electrode). Upon the electrodeposition of 2-ABA, the NH2-functionalized GQD/SPCE (fGQDs/SPCE) was washed sequentially with Milli-Q water and with PBS buffer (pH 7.4) and subsequently dried in air. The anti-AXL antibody was immobilized on the NH2 functionalized GQD/SPCE by dropping 10 μL of a 100 μg mL−1 anti-AXL solution prepared in 0.1 mol L−1 PBS solution of pH 7.4 containing 3 mg mL−1 sodium periodate, and incubating for 90 min at room temperature (25 ± 1 °C) in a humid ambient chamber. In this way, oriented antibody immobilization was achieved through stabilization of the Schiff bases between the amine functionalized GQDs/SPCE and the aldehyde groups generated in the antibody by oxidation of the carbohydrate residues [14–16]. After washing with PBS solution (pH 7.4), 10 μL of 5 mol L−1 sodium borohydride solution were dropped on the anti-AXL-fGQDs/SPCE and incubated for 30 min at 25 °C for further stabilization of the Schiff base. Subsequently, the immunosensor was thoroughly rinsed first with Milli-Q water and then with PBS buffer (pH 7.4) to remove unbound antibody molecules. Subsequently, 20 μL of 1% BSA (w/v) prepared in 0.1 mol L−1 PBS buffer (pH 7.4) were used to block the possible active binding sites on the electrode surface thus avoiding non-specific adsorptions. After 60 min incubation at room temperature and further washing with PBS buffer (pH 7.4), capture of the target analyte was accomplished during 90 min at room temperature by adding 10 μL of the AXL standard/sample solution (prepared in PBS). Finally, the AXL-anti-AXL-fGQDs/SPCE was rinsed with PBS buffer (pH 7.4) to remove unbounded AXL molecules prior the electrochemical measurements.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of AXL with the anti-AXL-fGQDs/SPCE immunosesnor
ce:section.ce:para: The determination of AXL was performed by immersing the immunosensor in a 5 mmol L −1 Fe(CN)6 3−/4− solution prepared in 0.1 mol L−1 KCl, and recording differential pulse voltammograms between −0.2 and + 0.6 V (vs. the Ag pseudo-reference electrode) at 50 mV s−1. The analytical outputs were obtained from the change in the DPV peak current before and after the antigen–antibody reaction. Between measurements, the immunosensor was washed sequentially with Milli-Q water and with PBS buffer (pH 7.4).

Human serum samples were provided from Hospital Clinic of Barcelona. These samples were analyzed with the anti-AXL-fGQDs/SPCE immunosensor after just a 2500-fold dilution with PBS (0.1 mol L−1, pH 7.4). Validation of the method was performed by comparison of the results provided by the immunosensor with those achieved using the commercial ELISA kit using the same immunoreagents.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization and surface morphology of GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Characterization of synthesized GQDs was made by using different techniques. The morphology of GQDs was tested by TEM and FESEM (Fig. 2). TEM images (Fig. 2A and B) show GQDs with uniform spherical shape and an average diameter of 8 ± 2 nm. The spherical shape observed is in agreement with that reported by other authors in the synthesis of GQDs by pyrolysis of citric acid [13]. The size distribution is relatively uniform although some nanoparticles may form aggregates due to the self-assembly of GQDs. Fig. 2 C shows the FESEM image confirming the uniform spherical shape and an average size of 10 ± 3 nm in diameter. The elemental mapping of GQDs shown in Figs. 2D and E confirmed the presence of C and O homogeneously dispersed across the whole material. Moreover, energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) measurements (Fig. 2F) verified that only C and O elements were present with a relative area of 0.83:0.17, thus confirming the successful synthesis of the GQDs.

The optical properties of synthesized GQDs were checked by UV–vis spectroscopy and PL. The spectra are shown in Figs. S1 and S2 of the Supporting Information. As it can be seen in Fig. S1, water soluble GQDs exhibit a broad absorption band around 360 nm related to the π- π* transition of aromatic C=C bonds (sp2 domains), while citric acid did not show any absorption peak in the UV–Vis domain. The PL spectra were recorded at different excitation wavelengths from 320 nm to 380 nm. Fig. S2 shows as the emission wavelength of GQDs is constant around 460 nm independently of the excitation wavelength used. This behavior can be attributed to the surface state distribution of sp2 clusters in GQDs, which are responsible for the fluorescence and particle size uniformity [12].

FTIR spectroscopy was used to characterize the functional groups (i.e. OH and COOH) of synthesized GQDs (Fig. S3). An absorption band appears at around 1692 cm_1 assignable to the C=O symmetric stretching vibration of the -COOH groups. The significant stretching vibration of aromatic C=C bond was observed at 1644 cm−1 indicating the formation of an aromatic product from the base material (citric acid). The broad peak at 3362 cm−1 can be assigned to the O–H stretching vibration from the carboxylic acid group. The weak doublet peak at 2861 and 2924 cm−1 are related to the symmetric and asymmetric stretching vibrations of CH2 group in alkyl hydrocarbon. The peaks observed at 1527 and 1415 cm−1 can be assigned to the asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibration of COO followed by C-O bonding at 1022 cm−1 [17].

X-ray diffraction was used to characterize the crystal structure of GQDs (Fig. S4). A single peak was observed around 2θ = 24° in the XRD pattern of GQDs belonging to the (002) crystal plane (JCPDS: 41–1487), indicating the formation of GQDs structure.

Finally, the redox behavior of GQDs at the GQDs/SPCE was also characterized by linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) (Fig. S5) revealing the appearance of a clear reduction peak at about −0.68 V which did not appear at a bare SPCE, thus confirming the reduction of the functional groups (e.g., OH, COOH) existing on the GQDs surface.

Fig. 3 displays the cyclic voltammograms recorded during the electropolymerization process of 2-ABA on the GQDs/SPCE. A electrochemically irreversible reduction peak (peak I) at a potential around −0.6 V was observed in the first cycle, which may be attributed to the reduction of aliphatic amine of 2-ABA at the GQDs/SPCE. Successive cycles gave rise to a significant decrease in the reduction current attributable to the formation of amide-carbonyl bonds via Lewis acid-alkaline reaction between the amine group of 2-ABA and carboxylic group of GQDs [17]. Cyclic voltammetry of 2-ABA on GQDs/SPCE also led to electrochemically oxidize the amine group of phenyl ring in the presence of aliphatic amine (selectively oxidized). The anodic and cathodic peaks of aromatic amine on 2-ABA (peak II and peak III) appeared in the −0.07 to −0.1 and −0.21 to −0.28 V ranges, respectively. Successive cycling produced an increase in these peak currents and a shift of the redox potential to more positive and more negative values for the oxidation and reduction processes, respectively. This behavior suggests that the polymerization of benzyl amine occurs through the nitrogen atom.

The different steps involved in the fabrication of the anti-AXL-fGQDs/SPCE were characterized by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and cyclic voltammetry (CV). Fig. 4 A shows the CVs recorded for bare SPCE, GQDs/SPCE, fGQDs/SPCE and anti-AXL-fGQDs/SPCE in 5.0 mM Fe(CN)6 3− /4- solution prepared in 0.1 M KCl. As it can be observed, the significant increase in the peak current of GQDs/SPCE (curve b) as well as in the degree of reversibility with respect to that observed at a bare SPCE (curve a), are in agreement with the excellent conductivity of GQDs and the expected enhancement of the electron transfer rate between the electrode surface and the redox probe at the interface. When the 2-ABA molecules were electropolymerized on the GQDs/SPCE surface (curve c), the peak currents largely increased probably due to the existence of protonated amine groups on the surface of the fGQDs. In fact, pKa values of amine groups in 2-ABA associated to the dissociation of amino group on the phenyl ring (aromatic amine) and aliphatic amine are 3.5 and 9.34, respectively. Therefore, at pH 7.4, there is a high proportion of positively charged molecules on the electrode surface, which facilitates the charge transfer process with the negative probe [18]. Finally, when the anti-AXL was immobilized onto fGQDs/SPCE (curve d), the peak currents remarkably decreased as a consequence of the lower conductivity provoked by the covalent immobilization of the antibody. Interestingly, the conductivity increased after the treatment of the anti-AXL-fGQDs/SPCE with borohydride solution and its further blocking with BSA (Fig. 4C) probably due to the replacement of some non-specifically adsorbed antibodies on the surface [19]. As expected, a decrease in the conductivity was observed after incubation with AXL (Fig. 4C). These results are in agreement with the decrease in the DPV peak observed after antigen-antibody immunoreactions used as transduction basics of the developed immunosensor.

Fig. 4B displays the corresponding Nyquist plots recorded at the same electrodes. As expected, the results obtained by EIS are in agreement with those observed by CV. A considerable decrease was apparent in the charge transfer resistance (Rct) when the SPCE was modified with the GQDs and further functionalized upon 2-ABA electropolymerization. In addition, and as expected, a significant increase in the Rct was observed after antibody immobilization. The Rct value for the bare SPCE was 2907 Ω, while this value at GQDs/SPCE and NH2-fGQDs/SPCE decreased to 679 and 19 Ω, respectively. In addition, when the antibody was immobilized on NH2-fGQDs/SPCE, the value of Rct increased to 1696 Ω. Consistently with the results achieved by CV, the Rct values observed after incubation with BSA and AXL were 561 and 737 Ω, respectively (Fig. 4D).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of the experimental variables involved in the preparation of the immunosensor
ce:section.ce:para: The different variables affecting the preparation of fGQDs/SPCEs and the performance of the AXL-anti-AXL-fGQDs/SPCE immunosensor were evaluated. The largest ratio (S/N) between the current differences (Δi) measured with the immunosensor in the absence (N) and for AXL standard solutions containing 100 pg mL−1 (S) was taken as the selection criterion for each tested variable. Optimization studies involved evaluation of the following variables: the GQDs loading used to modify the electrode surface; anti-AXL loading and its incubation time on the fGQDs/SPCEs, and concentration of blocking agent used and the corresponding incubation time. Details on these optimization studies are provided in the Supporting Information (Figs. S6–S8) and the obtained results summarized in Table S1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical characteristics for the determination of AXL achieved with the AXL-anti-AXL-fGQDs/SPCE immunosensor
ce:section.ce:para: Under the optimized experimental conditions, the plot of the DPV current differences in the absence and in the presence of AXL (Δi) versus the logarithm of AXL concentrations provided a linear (R2 = 0.994) behavior between 1.7 and 1000 pg mL−1 with slope and intercept values of (13.5 ± 0.6) μA and (8.5 ± 0.7) μA, respectively. It is important to note that this range is suitable for the determination of AXL in human serum after an appropriate dilution considering the established cut-off value (71 ng mL−1). According to the 3sb and 10sb criteria, where sb was estimated as the standard deviation (n = 10) for measurements in the absence of AXL, detection (LOD) and quantification limits of 0.5 and 1.7 pg mL−1, respectively, were estimated. Importantly, the achieved LOD is significantly lower than those reported for the two different electrochemical immunosensors mentioned above (74 pg mL−1 and 75 pg mL−1 using magnetic micro- and nano-particles, respectively [5] and 337 pg mL−1 for the integrated immunosensor using polypyrrole propionic acid-modified SPCEs [6]). The LOD is better also than the minimum detectable concentration achieved with commercial ELISA kits based on the use of the same immunoreagents, as example, 2 pg mL−1 was reported in the Human AXL ELISA kit from Thermo Fisher Scientific [20], which provides dynamic ranges usually covering from several tens to thousands of pg mL−1 AXL. Most likely, the high sensitivity of the immunosensor developed in this work can be attributed to the use of GQDs, which improved the kinetics of the electron transfer reaction and allowed the attachment of large antibody loadings because of the in-situ electrode functionalization with a high number of amine groups [10].

The reproducibility of the measurements was evaluated by measuring the Δi obtained for a 100 pg mL−1AXL standard solution with seven different immunosensors prepared on the same day and in the same manner. A relative standard deviation (RSD) value of 7.2% was calculated indicating an acceptable reproducibility for both the protocols used in the immunosensor preparation and the voltammetric transduction.

The storage stability of the as prepared anti-AXL-fGQDs/SPCE immune-platforms was evaluated. Different anti-AXL-fGQDs/SPCEs were prepared on the same day, stored at 4 °C in a humid environment and used in different days for measuring a 100 pg mL−1 standard AXL solution. Obtained results showed that the Δi value decreased 7.7 and 14.1% after 13 and 16 days of storage, respectively, thus suggesting a 15-days acceptable storage stability of the developed immunosensor.

The selectivity of the immunosensor was evaluated by comparing the Δi values and the corresponding S/N ratios measured for standards solutions of 0.0 and 100 pg mL−1 AXL prepared in the absence and in the presence of other non-target proteins such as cardiac troponin I (cTnI) and troponin T (cTnT), C-reactive protein (CRP), tumor necrosis factor (TNF), lipoprotein(a) (Lp(a)), and interleukin-8 (IL-8) at the usual concentrations they are found in the serum of healthy individuals. Fig. S9 (Supporting Information) shows that no significant differences were observed for both the Δi and S/N values in the absence and in the presence of all the non-target proteins, confirming the excellent selectivity of the immunosensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of sAXL in human serum
ce:section.ce:para: A paired samples t-test demonstrated that no significant differences (α = 0.05) existed between the results provided by the two methods (p-value = 0.46). Therefore, it could be concluded that the developed method using the immunosensor is reliable and involves only a simple dilution with PBS as sample treatment despite its complexity.

A very sensitive label-free immunosensor, using fGQDs-modified SPCEs as effective scaffolds for the immobilization of the capture antibody (anti-AXL) via Schiff reaction based chemistry, has been developed. The integrated immunosensor exhibits a very attractive analytical performance in terms of sensitivity, selectivity and stability. The achieved LOD, 0.5 pg mL−1, is significantly lower than those reported for the limited number of electrochemical immunosensors described so far in the literature for AXL determination. In addition, it is well below the cut-off value established (71 ng mL−1) to discriminate HF patients from healthy individuals, thus demonstrating the suitability of the method for clinical practice. Moreover, the analysis of human sera implies a minimal sample treatment (just a dilution with buffer) and the results obtained for real patients are in good agreement with those provided by a conventional ELISA kit. Compared to ELISAs, the developed methodology offers additional interesting advantages in terms of assay simplicity and required time as well as portability of the used instrumentation. It is worth to mention that the whole assay time (from anti-AXL-fGQDs/SPCE preparation to Δi measurement) is 90 min which is much shorter than that required for the ELISA method (4 h 40 min). In addition, the enhanced sensitivity achieved with the developed immunosensor (LOD of 0.5 pg mL−1) in comparison with those claimed for the commercial ELISA kit using the same immunoreagents (62.5 pg mL−1) and the other two electrochemical immunosensors for AXL determination reported so far (74–337 pg mL−1 [5,6]) allows the determination to be made using a significantly larger sample dilution (2500 vs 2–10 dilutions) with the advantages that this entails for matrix effect minimization and working with limited availability biological samples. These interesting features, together with the possibility of developing multiplexed detection, make this immunosensor a very attractive alternative to ELISAs for the development of automated POC systems for on-site determination of this powerful HF-associated biomarker and the methodology can be easily extended to the analysis of other relevant biomarkers.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Financial support from the University of Tabriz, Tabriz, Iran and of projects: Retos Colaboración RTC-2015-4184-1 (cofinanced by the Ministry of Economy and Competitivity and FEDER “una manera de hacer Europa”), CTQ2015-70023-R and CTQ2015-64402-C2-1-R (Spanish Ministry of Economy and Competitivity Research Projects) and S2013/MT-3029 (NANOAVANSENS Program from the Comunidad de Madrid) are gratefully acknowledged. The authors would also like to acknowledge Drs. M. Sabaté, M. Batlle and P. García de Frutos and the Hospital Clinic de Barcelona for kindly providing the serum samples from HF patients. Fariba Mollarasouli would like to thank her family for their valuable help and great effort.
